text: The surgeon lifts the stomach and performs a partial omentectomy along the greater curvature, extending towards the tip of the spleen. The left gastroepiploic vessels are isolated, ligated, and transected.
The surgeon cleans the stomach along the greater curvature to expose lymph node number four, D, while lifting the stomach for better access.

The surgeon moves to the right side of the patient to continue the partial omentectomy. Assistance provides traction on the mesocolon, pulling it downward and outward to facilitate dissection along the fusion embryology plane between the mesocolon and the greater omentum.

The assistant pulls down the pancreas to facilitate continued dissection along the common hepatic artery. The operator carefully manipulates the tissue around lymph node number eight to prevent injury to the pyloric branch of the hepatic artery.

The surgeon continues the dissection of lymph nodes along the common hepatic artery, moving towards the suprapancreatic border and extending to the splenic artery. This follows the dissection of the infrapyloric area.
The surgeon continues the dissection along the left gastric artery, exposing the coronary vein. The coronary vein is then ligated and transected.
The surgeon lifts the left gastric artery, applies double clips to secure it, and then transects the artery.
The surgeon returns to the infrapyloric area, where the bluish mesocolic vessels are visible. The dissection continues with the omentectomy procedure.

The surgeon carefully continues the partial omentectomy, progressing toward the second part of the duodenum while avoiding injury to the mesocortic vessel and transverse colon, particularly near the hepatic flexure.

The surgeon exposes the second part of the duodenum, with the assistant applying downward traction on the mesocolon. Gentle handling of the mesocolon and omentum allows for division at the fusion area.
The surgeon continues dissection along the mesocolon vessel towards the pancreas, using traction and counter-traction to safely identify the fusion plane between these structures.
The surgeon continues the omentectomy, dissecting towards the inferior border of the pancreas, with visible manipulation of the surrounding tissue.
The surgeon continues dissection along the right gastroepiploic vein and artery, carefully preserving the infrapyretic vessels while approaching the infrapyretic area.

The surgeon dissects the medial side of the right gastroepiploic vessels, ensuring no injury to the infrapyloric vessels. The infrapyloric vessel is visible below. Following the bifurcation, the right gastroepiploic vessels are ligated and transected.

The surgeon clears the greater curvature of the antrum, approximately five centimeters in length. Three gauzes are placed to expand the lesser omentum, facilitating the identification and dissection along the lesser curvature.
The surgeon begins dissection approximately three centimeters from the pylorus, focusing on the posterior side of the lesser omentum. The gastric arcade is exposed, ligated, and transected.

The surgeon visualizes the hepatic branch of the vagus nerve and begins removing lymph nodes along the number one area. The omentum is peeled off, and lymph nodes are carefully dissected away from the hepatic branch using the inactive blade of an energy device to prevent injury to underlying tissues.

The surgeon continues dissection along the cardia area, moving from the posterior to the anterior side. The lymphovascular tissue around the cardia and upper stomach is cleared, including the coronary vein and left gastric artery. The dissection progresses from the anterior to posterior side of the stomach, ensuring thorough clearance of lymphovascular structures.
The surgeon prepares for anastomosis by performing a vertical mini laparotomy to extract the stomach. The stomach is then transected three centimeters from the pylorus.

The surgeon sends a part of the stomach for frozen section confirmation. The tumor is localized, and proximal resection is performed on the greater curvature using an Allen clamp. Long linear staples are used to create the lesser curvature. The proximal stomach is prepared for anastomosis to the remnant distal antrum.
The surgeon performs an anastomosis between the proximal stomach and distal antrum using one layer of continuous interlocking sutures, ensuring a secure connection between the two sections.
